era_context: The mid-15th century was a period of significant political consolidation in Europe and Asia. The Ottoman Empire was expanding aggressively, the Italian Renaissance was flourishing, the Hundred Years' War was in its final stages, and the Ming Dynasty maintained stability in China. This was a transitional period between medieval and early modern political structures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited documentation)

events.title: Thirteen Years' War between Poland-Lithuania and Teutonic Order concludes
events.description: The Thirteen Years' War (1454–1466) was ongoing in 1457, with Poland-Lithuania gaining the upper hand against the declining Teutonic Order. This conflict would result in the Peace of Thorn (1459), which significantly weakened the Order and established Poland-Lithuania as a major Baltic power. The war marked the beginning of the end for the Teutonic Order as a political force in Eastern Europe.
key_figures: Ludwig von Vietinghoff (Grand Master of the Teutonic Order)
events.certainty_note: The Thirteen Years' War is well-documented in Polish and Teutonic Order archives. The date range is certain.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet era effectively ends
events.description: By 1457, the Ming Dynasty had effectively ended its large-scale maritime exploration expeditions. The treasure fleet voyages, which had been sponsored by the Ming court under Zheng He (who died in 1433), were no longer being funded or organized. This marked a significant shift in Ming strategic priorities away from maritime expansion toward land-based defense against Mongol threats. The end of these expeditions represented a missed opportunity for Chinese global dominance.
events.certainty_note: The decline of treasure fleet expeditions is well-documented in Ming records, though the exact year of cessation varies slightly across sources (1433–1460).
cross_references: Part of broader Ming strategic reorientation

events.title: Restoration of Emperor Zhengtong (Tianshun) in Ming China
events.description: Emperor Zhengtong was restored to the Ming throne in 1457 after being deposed in 1449 during the Tumu Crisis. His restoration, known as the Tianshun restoration, marked a return to stability after a period of internal conflict. Zhengtong (now known as Tianshun) ruled until 1464, overseeing a period of relative stability and consolidation of Ming power.
events.certainty_note: The restoration of Zhengtong is well-documented in Ming records. The date is certain.
cross_references: Follows from 1449-001 (Tumu Crisis and Zhengtong's deposition)

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian tensions escalate in the Aegean
events.description: Tensions between the Ottoman Empire and Venice were escalating in the Aegean in 1457, as the Ottomans sought to consolidate control over Mediterranean trade routes and island territories. These tensions would erupt into open war in 1463 (First Venetian-Ottoman War). The conflict represented the broader struggle between the declining Venetian maritime republic and the rising Ottoman Empire.
key_figures: Venetian Senate and merchant republic leadership

sources.name: Venetian state archives and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Pre-war tensions are documented in Venetian records, though specific incidents in 1457 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ashikaga shogunate instability in Japan
events.description: The Ashikaga shogunate continued to experience internal instability and fragmentation of authority in 1457. Competing daimyo factions were vying for power, and the shogun's authority was increasingly challenged. This period of instability would eventually lead to the Sengoku (Warring States) period, which began in 1467 with the Onin War.

sources.name: Onin War chronicles and later Japanese historical compilations

events.certainty_note: The general political instability of this period is well-documented, though specific events within 1457 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Thirteen Years' War is sometimes dated as 1454–1466, but some sources extend it to 1459 (Peace of Thorn). The exact year of the war's conclusion is somewhat conventional. The restoration of Emperor Zhengtong in 1457 is well-documented, but some sources refer to him as Tianshun after his restoration, which can cause confusion. The end of the treasure fleet era is variously dated to 1433 (Zheng He's death), 1450s, or 1460s depending on the source.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1457 is rich in European sources, particularly from Poland-Lithuania, Venice, and the Ottoman Empire. Chinese sources are abundant but focus on court-centered events. Japanese sources are available but often later compilations. African and American sources are virtually absent from the surviving written record. The bias toward political and military events is pronounced; economic, cultural, and social developments are less systematically recorded. Ottoman sources are often later compilations rather than contemporary records.

graph_edges.note: The Thirteen Years' War led to the Peace of Thorn in 1459.
The end of the treasure fleet era followed Zheng He's death in 1433.
Zhengtong's restoration followed his deposition in 1449 during the Tumu Crisis.
Escalating Ottoman-Venetian tensions erupted into open war in 1463.
The political instability of the Ashikaga shogunate led to the Onin War in 1467.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
